ORDER DENYING PLAINTIFF FIREMAN FUND INSURANCE COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF FIREMAN'S FUND INSURANCE COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION AS TO THE FIRST CAUSE OF ACTION FOR EXPRESS CONTRACTUAL INDEMNITY FOR DEFENSE COSTS IS DENIED AS TO ALL ISSUES. THERE DOES NOT APPEAR TO BE A PROBLEM WITH TRITON SUBS, INC.'S CORPORATE STATUS IN CALIFORNIA. PLAINTIFF FAILED TO MAINTAIN ITS SUMMARY ADJUDICATION BURDEN THAT IT IS ENTITLED TO INDEMNITY FOR THE DEFENSE COSTS. COLORADO LAW APPLIES SINCE SECTION 19.1 OF THE MARKETING AGREEMENT CONTAINS A VALID AND ENFORCEABLE CHOICE OF LAW PROVISION. PARAGRAPH 18.4 OF THE AGREEMENT PROVIDES FOR A DUTY TO INDEMNIFY, NOT A DUTY TO DEFEND. UNDER COLORADO LAW, THE DUTY TO INDEMNIFY IS SEPARATE AND DISTINCT FROM THE DUTY TO DEFEND. (CYPRUS AMAX MINERALS CO. V. LEXINGTON INS. CO. (CO 2003) 74 P.3D 294, 299.) THE DUTY TO DEFEND IS CONTRACTUAL IN NATURE AND MUST BE EXPLICIT IN THE TERMS OF THE CONTRACT. (THOMPSON V. MARYLAND CASUALTY CO. (CO 2004) 84 P.3D 496, 501.) IT EXISTS IN THE INSURANCE CONTEXT WHEN THE UNDERLYING COMPLAINT "ALLEGES ANY FACTS THAT MIGHT FALL WITHIN THE COVERAGE OF THE POLICY, EVEN IF ALLEGATIONS ONLY POTENTIALLY OR ARGUABLY FALL WITHIN THE POLICY'S COVERAGE." (ID., CITATIONS OMITTED.) IN CONTRAST, A DUTY TO INDEMNIFY ARISES ONLY WHEN THE INSURANCE "POLICY ACTUALLY COVERS THE HARM." (COTTER CORP. V. AMERICAN EMPIRE SURPLUS LINES INS. CO. (COLO. 2004) 90 P.3D 814, 830). PLAINTIFF PROVIDES FACTS IN ITS SEPARATE STATEMENT OUTLINING THE ALLEGATIONS MADE IN THE UNDERLYING COMPLAINTS AND THE ARGUMENTS MADE IN TRIAL BRIEFS, BUT NO FACTS SHOWING THAT TRITON'S NEGLIGENCE ACTUALLY CAUSED HARM THAT IS COVERED BY THE INDEMNIFICATION AGREEMENT. FOR EXAMPLE, THERE ARE NO FACTS REGARDING THE TERMS OF A JUDGMENT AGAINST TRITON OR A STIPULATION ESTABLISHING LIABILITY BY TRITON. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; REPORTER: SHERRY SAWYER, CSR #5976 =302/HK

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS TRITON SUBS, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND ALTERNATIVE MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ARE DENIED IN THEIR ENTIRETY. TRITON'S MOTION IS BASED ENTIRELY ON THE ARGUMENT THAT IT WAS FOUND TO BE 0% AT FAULT IN THE UNDERLYING CASE. HOWEVER, THE VERDICT FORM IN THE UNDERLYING CASE DOES NOT ESTABLISH THAT TRITON LACKED ANY FAULT FOR THE FIRE. THE VERDICT FORM SHOWS THAT THE COMBINED PARTIES OF "TRITON SUBS, INC./GORHAM" WERE NEGLIGENT AND THAT THEIR NEGLIGENCE WAS A SUBSTANTIAL FACTOR IN CAUSING THE FIRE, YET THAT NEGLIGENCE OCCURRED WITHIN THE SCOPE OF THEIR AGENCY AS AN AGENT OF QUIZNOS AND THUS, PER THE DIRECTIONS ON THE VERDICT FORM, THE JURY WAS INSTRUCTED TO AND DID ASSIGN ALL OF THE FAULT ATTRIBUTABLE TO TRITON/GORHAM TO QUIZNOS. THE JURY'S DETERMINATION OF 0% FAULT OF TRITON/GORHAM DOES NOT COLLATERALLY ESTOP PLAINTIFF FIREMAN'S FUND INSURANCE COMPANY FROM ASSERTING THAT TRITON WAS AT FAULT FOR CAUSING THE FIRE BECAUSE THE ISSUE OF TRITON'S FAULT IN THIS CASE IS NOT THE SAME OR IDENTICAL TO THE ISSUE DECIDED BY THE JURY. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED AND FILED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: GORDON F. AIAVAO, CSR NO. 11216, EMAIL: GAIAVAO@SFTC.ORG. (D302)

Defendants Triton Subs, Inc., Robert Gorham, Suzanne Santia Gorham, Mark Rogers' Motion For Summary Judgment And Alternatively Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Aug-08-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Tim Harris of the law offices of Charleston Revich & Chamberlin LLP (310-551-7002) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Gregory J. Sowder of the law offices of Harrington Foxx Dubrow & Canter LLP (415-288-6600) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party, Triton Subs, Inc.

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

Defendants Triton Subs, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication are DENIED in their entirety. Triton's motion is based entirely on the argument that it was found to be 0% at fault in the underlying case. However, the verdict form in the underlying case does not establish that Triton lacked any fault for the fire. The verdict form shows that the combined parties of "Triton Subs, Inc./Gorham" were negligent and that their negligence was a substantial factor in causing the fire, yet that negligence occurred within the scope of their agency as an agent of Quiznos and thus, per the directions on the verdict form, the jury was instructed to and did assign all of the fault attributable to Triton/Gorham to Quiznos. The jury's determination of 0% fault of Triton/Gorham does not collaterally estop plaintiff Fireman's Fund Insurance Company from asserting that Triton was at fault for causing the fire because the issue of Triton's fault in this case is not the same or identical to the issue decided by the jury. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Nov-29-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. STEPHEN P. SOSKIN OF THE CHARLSTON ROVICH & WOLLITZ LLP, TEL. NO. 1-310-551-7032 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. GREGORY J. SOWDER OF THE HARRINGTON, FOXX, DUBROW & CANTER, LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-288-6600 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; PLAINTIFF FIREMAN'S FUND INSURANCE COMPANY'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION as to the first cause of action for express contractual indemnity for defense costs is DENIED as to all issues. There does not appear to be a problem with Triton Subs, Inc.'s corporate status in California. Plaintiff failed to maintain its summary adjudication burden that it is entitled to indemnity for the defense costs. Colorado law applies since section 19.1 of the Marketing Agreement contains a valid and enforceable choice of law provision. Paragraph 18.4 of the Agreement provides for a duty to indemnify, not a duty to defend. Under Colorado law, the duty to indemnify is separate and distinct from the duty to defend. (Cyprus Amax Minerals Co. v. Lexington Ins. Co. (CO 2003) 74 P.3d 294, 299.) The duty to defend is contractual in nature and must be explicit in the terms of the contract. (Thompson v. Maryland Casualty Co. (CO 2004) 84 P.3d 496, 501.) It exists in the insurance context when the underlying complaint "alleges any facts that might fall within the coverage of the policy, even if allegations only potentially or arguably fall within the policy's coverage." (Id., citations omitted.) In contrast, a duty to indemnify arises only when the insurance "policy actually covers the harm." (Cotter Corp. v. American Empire Surplus Lines Ins. Co. (Colo. 2004) 90 P.3d 814, 830). Plaintiff provides facts in its separate statement outlining the allegations made in the underlying complaints and the arguments made in trial briefs, but no facts showing that Triton's negligence actually caused harm that is covered by the indemnification agreement. For example, there are no facts regarding the terms of a judgment against Triton or a stipulation establishing liability by Triton. Order signed in open court. DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-22-2015 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. STEPHEN P. SOSKIN OF THE CHARLSTON, REVICH & WOLLITZ LLP, TEL. NO. 1-310-551-7000 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. GREGORY J. SOWDER OF THE HARRINGTON, FOXX, DUBROW & CANTER LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-288-6600 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT TRITON SUBS, INC.'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is overruled. Paragraph 18.4 of the contract [indemnification] states that Triton Subs, Inc. agrees to indemnify Quiznos for liability as well as damages. Globe Indem. Co. v. Larkin (1944) 62 Cal.App.2d 891, 893-894, is dispositive and explains that where an indemnification provision has those dual aspects, a cause of action runs from the payment of damages. There is no allegation in the complaint regarding when plaintiff made the payments and plaintiff is not obligated to plead around a tenuous statute of limitations defense. The court grants judicial notice of the verdict but nothing in the verdict demonstrates that plaintiff cannot state a cause of action. Order signed in open court. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter : Melanie Gheno, CSR # 7489 =302/eg
